item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Musical nitpick: I'd imagine you could get far more than 12 variations of the melody given that there are infinite tones in between our 440Hz-based chromatic notes. I think it'd be more accurate to simply leave the part about it being the same regardless of tonic without explicitly naming a finite number of variations.

Besides that, I'm not sure I really understand a couple of the details of this piece. Why is CAOCIT mentioned only in the red revision-changes, except in Addendum 1, where it's seemingly discussed in the pre-revision document for no reason? I'm also left confused by the inconsistent redactions throughout the piece, which seem to be disconnected from the anomaly, except for the general loss of identity the researcher in the time box feels.

I did really enjoy the audio files you included. The way you used the motifs in them was an enjoyable exercise. While I imagine the foundation would be able to collect some examples of more varied genre/instrumentation, I can't fault you for that; you'd need some other musicians involved or a really uncommon breadth of musical skill.

I'll have to sit on this one for a bit. I'm drawn to this as a musician who's fond of puzzles, but I do feel like this one's a bit opaque, and there's not a lot of obvious story to make it feel emotionally engaging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I did a very detailed breakdown of my thoughts, but it's taking a bit to cohesively put them together, so I'll summarize:

+ show block
– hide block

The notes at the end are from the perspective of the Doctor who decided to enter the Time Box - the Misters being referred to are the respective computers, and the reason that this is Apollyon is because it's inevitable that any AI whose goal is to create music will replicate a specific song that matches the name of an abstract entity in deep-space, which will cause the universe to swap properties with that entity and thus become abstracted and unreal.

What's interesting is that he redacted everything BEFORE entering the box, and he gives his reasoning in that very first Addendum note (which happens to describe LOGICIAN from SCP-INTEGER.) That entire addendum section is being written while he's in the box, and he slowly goes insane. Before he loses everything entirely, he desperately tries to save the changes he's made… which fail because he doesn't have access privileges. Which he lost when he lost his identity in the box.

This whole skip is a tribute to SCP-INTEGER… Placeholder's very first skip :D

Edit: I really like how you snuck Placeholder's name in, but I am not certain how relevant that is, other than a fun easter egg.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this SCP, and like all DoMc SCPs I'm unable to adequately explain any of it. The short compositions are a lot of fun too. Strong +1.

Small nitpick: in the description, it said the melody doesn't fit into any popularly known scale. This isn't correct because it fits into the 12-tone or chromatic scale (unless there's something going on with microtonal variation that's not transcribed). It would be more accurate to say it doesn't reflect any common diatonic scale.

Overall, the melody isn't unusual, it's actually pretty simple and really pleasant, which adds to the mystery I think. I tried to deal music analysis, but no description fits neatly. It's simply C major with some chromatic variation. Overall, well done.

item.title: AI's favorite scale
item.content:encoded: I noticed the melody wasn't in any conventional key signatures, So I tried to look up what scale it is. The notes shown in chromatic order are: C, C#, F, G, A#, and B. Starting from C, the scale would Half step, Major 3rd, Whole step, Minor 3rd, Half step, Half step. allthescales.org lists it as the 16th 6-note scale Pothimic mode. Congrats on sending me on a scavenger hunt. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one hooked me at the start, what with the format screw and music, but as I read on, it just didn't reel me in at all. The bit about space signals and the log of someone going insane didn't really "hit", even after a second read of the article. While this definitely feels appropriately confusing for a department of miscommunications article, that alone doesn't make an article interesting or enjoyable to read. There's clear effort here, and it did do a decent job getting me to read it all the way through, but with the cons I mentioned earlier I'll be giving this a novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting for the phat bangers in the addenda.

item.title: Music makes you lose control - Declass attempt
item.content:encoded:
The article is well written, but I find the narrative hard to understand. I think an official declassification would be very good.

+ Attempt at D-class-if-cation
- Probably wrong in some places

The red portions of the article indicate a change from the original article. Let's look at the original text first.

Originally, two personnel talk about the technology and how SCP-3955 is recorded. By putting a music creating AI in a WARP Train from Library of Ruina box that accelerates time, SCP-3955 will be observed in the created music. A few examples are provided, and the article ends.

Except for the mysterious edits that have been placed in the article. SCP-3955 doesn't need to be contained or concealment, but its now an Apollyon class SCP. Whoever is editing this article doesn't want the anomaly to be referred as an SCP. Re-reading the article, it seems like ██████ ████ (one of the scientists behind the anomaly) is trying to stop a rogue AI (the AI being tested to make music) from changing the document.

██████ ████ has a portion of their text in the Conprocs, as there's a note that unauthorized changes are to be reverted immediately. To me, this edit in the Conprocs is very different from the other edits. Potentially, they might be losing control of the entire article in addendum 03. This addendum was made when the article was last updated, and the editor's words begin to become heavily blackboxed. Quote:

"On April 12th, at 4:02 pm, Dr. ████ followed up on a hunch regarding ███-3955's possible connections to a series of abstract, deep-space transmissions found in CRYSTAL-INE's database. Findings indicate ████ ██ ███ ██ █████ █████ ██████ ██ ████ ███ ███ ████ █████████ ██████ ███ █████ ██ █ ████ ██ █████.

There are to be no further additions to this document."

Once the AI takes control of the article, it starts heavily blackboxing everything related to the SCP Foundation. The reason for this is that the AI has gained some sentience. This is evident in addendum 04, where the AI begins to feel emotion and develops advanced thoughts. At first, it has its basic functions listed and it begins to become wary of its purpose. It starts going insane and starts questioning music, even giving names to its "cyclical" music processes. Once the AI finds the document, it tries to change it dramatically, even putting a warning in the Conprocs to stop testing. Due to its lack of access privileges, the article remains in its editing phase.

…Re-reading this, the rogue AI thin is probably all incorrect. It's just strange that edits are being made after 16:02, as addendum 04 has a log from 16:04. If this really is an Apollyon, is it talking about how music is unstoppable? Or did the AI find out that SCP-3955 is very dangerous for humans?

Probably wasted time writing that, but eh. +1

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: dohero, instead of posting multiple comments in succession, please edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That helps prevent spam.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh ok. sorry if it was a bad critique, im still pretty new here. ill reread this and try and figure it out

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh ok. sorry if it was a bad critique, im still pretty new here

item.title: Re: Well written, kept my interest -- just two problems
item.content:encoded: The letters are censored for a story reason. They're part of the format screw that's part of the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I can't explain why it's apollyon or what the red text is without spoiling the answer to "what's really going on" but everything in this piece is intentional. It's a DoMC piece, which tend to be format-screwy and weird, so if something seems out of place, it's likely because it's supposed to catch your eye.

item.title: Re: Well written, kept my interest -- just two problems
item.content:encoded:
Every catalogued skip has those letters in the listing.

Not even close.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A few things. One is the copious amount of red, its a bit jarring and seems very unnecessary so I would cut that if I were you. Two, this thing isn't Apollyon. It is inevitable yes, but its not going to end the world, and its effects can be easily contained by supressing AI research into the field of music and not letting people listen. Also I feel that there has to be a better reason for it to be an "anomaly," constants in AI happen all of the time and I don't see why this one is any different. I also don't see why the SCP number is censored, as as far as I could tell the article had no mention of the need to censor that. That ties in to another issue I had, with the Department of Miscommunications. That feels just tacked on, and it serves no narrative purpose. Right now I am downvoting, but this thing has potential I think.

item.title: Well written, kept my interest -- just two problems
item.content:encoded:
• "Biproduct" should be corrected to "by-product".
  • Why is the "SCP" prefix, rather than the identification number, censored? Every catalogued skip has those letters in the listing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My review on the Art Exchange forum:

This is some mind-bendy Department of Miscommunications shit (yay, another baby of mine that has a life of its own on this site, love seeing these things crop up — though before you think me rude let me be the first to admit that it may be my baby but I am not the only parent) about music! I critiqued this before it got sent out, and I am glad to see the changes that happened. My favorite lines are still in here (Mr. Measure, a nice middle note) but a lot of what I felt was the more generic "going crazy" stuff is gone. I really have no idea what is happening in this skip (even though I know the Misters are the three computers), but I do really like it. Fits the DoMc vibe perfectly.

My opinion hasn't changed. +1

item.title: best December 25th of my life
item.content:encoded:
Boy, am I ever so lucky to be blessed with this gift. I asked for something complex and puzzle-y relating to music and one of my other interests. Henzoid went above and beyond, including elements of DoMC, AI, math, surrealism, esoteric classes, and actual composed snippets. I'm still figuring this one out, and it still totally blows me away. I'm excited to declass it soon; keep your eyes out for that.

Thank you to DarkStuff and Elogee for organizing the Art Exchange and for assigning me such a talented and like-minded gifter. Thank you Henzoid for being awesome. Thank you to pinch-hitters for making sure everyone enjoys the holiday season.

Merry Christmas, Happy Holidays, Wishful Winters, Super Solstice Season, etc.

item.title: Happy Giftmas!
item.content:encoded:
Gravity drags me deep beneath the cavity in the rocky sand
Blue turns to pitch black as I sink into the land
Gravity renders me a ragdoll, tumbling to the chasm floor
Looking up at the ceiling miles below the shore

Licensing Stuff
Boo, did I scare ya?

All images and music made by me, Henzoid.

Huh, I guess I didn't really need a collapsible to say that, but here we are.

Thanks Licensing Team! You guys rock.

This SCP was written in honor of the lovely Placeholder McD for the Holiday Gift-Swap. This gift was super fun to write, in part because Placeholder and I have near-identical taste in SCPs. In fact, though the main premise is brand new, I've been meaning to do a music SCP like this for quite some time, but never found the motivation. But, enough about me, here's to Placeholder MCD: An incredible author, and a lovely human being. Cheers!

Thanks to my gorgeous critters:
- Crocket_Lawnchair
- DarkStuff
- Brewsterion 1017
- Elunerazim
- Yossipossi
